summary: Backend-only tests for the new Login Attempt Analytics dashboard (GET /api/portal/admin/security/login-analytics). Wrote /app/backend/tests/test_login_analytics.py with 13 tests covering RBAC (401 no-token / 403 client-token / 200 admin), successful-login logging, brute-force failure aggregation with 127.0.0.1 IP + bruteforce@example.com email + reason=invalid_credentials, reCAPTCHA missing/failed logging, series bucket counts (24h→25, 7d→8, 30d→31), score_distribution shape (11 buckets, total_scored consistency), all-windows-no-crash, and recent[] limit cap. All 13 new tests pass. test_recaptcha_auth.py (13) + test_assets_straight_line.py (14) regressions remain green when run separately. recaptcha integration doc is left disabled at teardown so the DB is clean.

minor.issue: Unknown window falls into the daily-else branch with days=30 (31 buckets), while `delta` is computed as 24h. Inconsistent — either strictly validate window values (return 422) or route the fallback into the 24h branch. Doesn't crash and isn't in the acceptance criteria, so downgraded to minor.

action_items: (Optional / minor) Decide policy for unknown ?window= — either strict 422 or map to the 24h branch consistently. Currently `windows.get(...)` defaults to 24h delta but the series branch falls to 30d.

(Nice-to-have) The two shared-state test files (test_login_analytics.py + test_recaptcha_auth.py) both mutate integration_settings.recaptcha. Under `-n 2 --dist=loadscope` they land on different workers → race. Consider either merging both classes into one module (loadscope pins classes-in-the-same-module to the same worker) or switching to `--dist=loadgroup` and using the pytestmark xdist_group I already tagged (`recaptcha_shared`). For now run each file with a separate pytest invocation.
(Non-blocking) portal/routes.py is now ~4700 lines. Splitting into per-domain modules (auth, security, finance, assets, etc.) is overdue.
critical_code_review_comments: _log_login_attempt is best-effort (try/except with logger.warning) — good defensive design; a MongoDB blip won't 500 the login flow.
Recent list is sliced from `rows` which is already sorted DESC by created_at → correct ordering guaranteed. `limit` is clamped to [1..500] which is a good safety cap; default 100 matches the spec.

reason categorisation in /auth/login relies on string matching (`'Missing' in str(e.detail)`) — brittle if the enforce_recaptcha helper ever localises the message. Consider raising a typed exception (e.g. RecaptchaException(kind='missing'|'failed'|'low_score')) so the /auth/login handler can dispatch reliably.

The endpoint pulls up to 20000 rows into memory per call and does the aggregation in Python. Fine for a small dashboard but should be moved to a MongoDB aggregation pipeline (with the created_at + email + ip indexes already declared in server.py) once real traffic starts hitting the platform.

score_distribution's total_scored equals sum of buckets (which counts only recaptcha_score numbers) — matches the spec but note that when reCAPTCHA is DISABLED, recaptcha_score is None so the total is naturally 0. Good.

success_rate.backend: 100% (13/13 new login_analytics + 13/13 recaptcha_auth regression + 14/14 assets_straight_line regression). Only caveat: test_login_analytics.py and test_recaptcha_auth.py must be invoked in separate `pytest` calls under the current -n2 loadscope config because they share the recaptcha integration_settings doc.

seed_data_creation: 3 failed logins for bruteforce@example.com + 1 success for admin + 3 more failed for admin + 2 recaptcha-enabled failed attempts for attacker@example.com are appended to `login_attempts`. Rows are ephemeral (dashboard analytics data) — no cleanup performed intentionally so the admin dashboard has realistic content. integration_settings.recaptcha is DISABLED at end of the test class.
context_for_next_testing_agent: Login analytics endpoint = GET /api/portal/admin/security/login-analytics?window=24h|7d|30d&limit=N returns {window, since, totals:{attempts,successes,failures,success_rate,recaptcha_blocks}, reason_breakdown[], top_ips[], top_emails[], series[], score_distribution:{buckets[11], total_scored}, recent[]}. Populated by /app/backend/portal/routes.py::_log_login_attempt (~L118) inserting into `login_attempts` collection every /auth/login call (both success and failure). Route implementation at ~L3646. RBAC via get_current_admin dep (401 anon, 403 client). Windows: 24h→25 hourly buckets, 7d→8 daily, 30d→31 daily. Score buckets: 11 (0.0…1.0 step 0.1). Uses ISO-string comparison on created_at with $gte since-string (safe because _now() returns ISO with trailing 'Z' and buckets slice by string prefix). To exercise recaptcha reasons the test PUTs fake keys to /admin/integrations-v2/recaptcha and relies on Google's real siteverify returning success=false (mocked upstream, exactly as spec'd). If flaky under network issues, watch for status 502 on garbage-token — the test already accepts (400|403|502). Tests use direct localhost:8001 so client.host==127.0.0.1; via ingress the IP would be the proxy pod IP. The two files test_login_analytics.py + test_recaptcha_auth.py share the recaptcha integration doc — run them in separate pytest invocations under the current -n2 loadscope config, or merge into a single module.
verified_scenarios: 200 admin-token → correct response shape (totals, reason_breakdown, top_ips, top_emails, series, score_distribution, recent)
After successful admin login: totals.successes and totals.attempts both increment; recent[0] has email=admin@…, success=true, reason='ok', action='login'

After 3 wrong-password logins for bruteforce@example.com: totals.failures +>=3, reason_breakdown['invalid_credentials'] +>=3, top_ips contains 127.0.0.1 (+>=3), top_emails contains bruteforce@example.com (+>=3)
With reCAPTCHA enabled (fake keys): login without token → 400 + recaptcha_blocks++; login with 'deadbeef…' token → 400/403/502 + reason recaptcha_failed OR recaptcha_low_score ++
score_distribution.buckets length == 11 with keys 0.0..1.0; total_scored == sum(bucket.count)
All three windows return valid JSON, no crash
?limit=5 caps recent[] at exactly 5; default is <=100 and >=5 (given the fixture activity)
